page.text: Oil tankers transport some 2,000 million tonnes of crude oil around the world by sea. Most of the time, oil is transported safely and without problems. The IMO introduced measures to ensure that most oil tankers are safely built and operated and are constructed to limit the amount of oil spilled in the case of an accident. Operational pollution, like for instance from routine tank cleaning operations, has also been cut. The first oil tankers appeared in the late 19th century. From the 1950s onwards, tankers grew rapidly in size. The first 100,000-tonne crude oil tanker was built in 1959 to cover the route from Europe to the Middle East round the Cape of Good Hope, thereby avoiding the Suez Canal which had been temporarily closed after the political conflicts in 1956. Shippers saw economies of scale in larger tankers and from the mid-1960s onwards VLCCs or Very Large Crude Carriers, tankers of more than 150,000 tonnes deadweight, were taken into service.

The potential for oil to pollute the marine environment was officially recognised by the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution of the Sea by Oil (OILPOL). The OILPOL Convention recognised that most oil pollution resulted from routine shipboard operations such as the cleaning of cargo tanks. At the time it was normal practice to wash the tanks out with water and then pump the resulting mixture of oil and water into the sea. The OILPOL Convention prohibited the dumping of oily wastes within a certain distance from land and in certain special areas where no dumping at sea is allowed. Meanwhile the IMO also set up a Subcommittee on Oil Pollution, under the auspices of the Maritime Safety Committee, to address oil pollution issues. Although the OILPOL Convention had been ratified, pollution control still remained a minor concern as the world was only beginning to wake up to the environmental consequences of an industrialised society.

But in April 1967, the Torrey Canyon ran aground when entering the English Channel and spilled her complete cargo of crude oil (120,000 tonnes) into the sea. This resulted in the biggest oil pollution incident ever recorded up to that time. It was essentially this incident that set in motion the chain of events that finally led to the adoption of MARPOL and to a series of Conventions regarding liability and compensation.
